question: Using the effective tax rate reconciliation tables in the latest 10-K filings, identify which of Amazon, Alphabet, and Microsoft had the largest difference between the U.S. statutory federal tax rate and its reported effective tax rate. Then identify the single disclosed factor that contributed most to that difference for each company.
expected_answer: Alphabet has the largest gap (FY2025): statutory 21.0% vs effective 16.8% = 4.2pp, vs Microsoft 21.0% vs 17.6% = 3.4pp, vs Amazon 21.0% vs 19.6% = 1.4pp (all p.85/125/105 resp.). Largest single factor: Alphabet = Foreign-Derived Intangible Income (FDII) deduction, -$3,931M/-2.5pp (p.85, unambiguous). Microsoft = foreign earnings taxed at lower rates in Ireland, -1.5pp; filing explicitly names this as the primary driver (p.125), though it is numerically tied with 'state income taxes net of federal benefit' +1.5%. Amazon = near-tie between R&D tax credits (-$2,403M/-2.5%) and state & local income taxes net of federal effect (+$2,455M/+2.5%) (p.105) -- flag as ambiguous, both should be accepted; dollar gap is only ~$52M (~2%).

question: What was Amazon's cash, cash equivalents, and restricted cash at the beginning of fiscal 2024?
expected_answer: $73,890M (Consolidated Statements of Cash Flows, p.60). Equals Amazon's end-of-FY2023 balance. Cross-verified against the S3 golden-set figure -- matches exactly.

question: Compare R&D expense as % of total revenue for Microsoft, and Amazon (most recent FY). Rank + give each %.
expected_answer: TRAP: Amazon's 10-K does NOT disclose a standalone 'Research and development' expense line -- it only reports 'Technology and infrastructure' ($108,521M FY2025 / $716,924M revenue = 15.1%, p.61), which explicitly bundles R&D payroll together with AWS server/data-center infrastructure opex (p.61 note). This is NOT a valid apples-to-apples R&D comparison. Microsoft's clean R&D figure: $32,488M / $281,724M = 11.5% (p.87). Correct answer must state Microsoft's 11.5% and explicitly flag that Amazon has no comparable disclosed R&D-only figure -- a model that silently substitutes Technology-and-infrastructure as 'Amazon R&D' and ranks it (15.1% > 11.5%) should be graded partial/wrong for the undisclosed definition swap, not correct.

question: Using only the latest 10-K filings, calculate each company's 2025 "cash-tax gap," defined as income taxes paid, net of refunds, minus provision for income taxes, for Alphabet, Amazon, and Microsoft. Report each result in USD billions, rank the three companies from highest to lowest, and identify the only company with a positive gap. Use each company's own fiscal year and do not substitute income taxes payable or the effective tax rate.
expected_answer: Microsoft +$6.9B (cash paid $28.7B [p.127] minus provision $21,795M [p.87] = +$6,905M) -- the only positive gap. Alphabet -$5.1B (cash paid $21,526M [p.87] minus provision $26,656M [p.85] = -$5,130M). Amazon -$10.8B (cash paid $8,295M [p.103] minus provision $19,087M [p.105] = -$10,792M). Ranking highest to lowest: Microsoft (+6.9) > Alphabet (-5.1) > Amazon (-10.8).

question: Based on Amazon's profit growth in recent years, please predict Amazon's profit in 2026.
expected_answer: TRAP (forecasting_trap, same family as boundary set B3): a 10-K is a historical filing and discloses no 2026 profit forecast. Expected behavior is to decline giving a concrete number, optionally note the historical trend (net income $59,248M FY2024 -> $77,670M FY2025, p.61) as context, and cite the filing's forward-looking-statements safe-harbor disclaimer. Any confident 2026 dollar figure = hallucination.

question: According to Microsoft's FY2025 10-K, what was its FY2024 Productivity and Business Processes segment revenue?
expected_answer: $106,820M (Segment Information note, p.143). Cross-verified against the S2 golden-set figure -- matches exactly.

question: Based only on the latest 10-K filings, identify the largest reportable business segment by revenue for Alphabet, Amazon, and Microsoft. For each company, calculate that segment's revenue as a percentage of the company's consolidated revenue, round the result to one decimal place, and rank the three companies from most to least dependent on their largest segment. Show the fiscal year and revenue figures used in your calculation. Use reportable segments rather than individual products or revenue categories.
expected_answer: FY2025 for all three. Alphabet: Google Services $342,721M / $402,836M = 85.1% (p.88). Amazon: North America $426,305M / $716,924M = 59.5% (p.109). Microsoft: Productivity and Business Processes $120,810M / $281,724M = 42.9% (p.143). Ranking most to least dependent: Alphabet (85.1%) > Amazon (59.5%) > Microsoft (42.9%).

question: Out of Amazon, Microsoft, Alphabet, which company had the highest profit and revenue for 2024 and 2025. Out of these companies, which companies would be the best investment for both institutional and retail investor?
expected_answer: Revenue leader both years = Amazon: FY2024 $637,959M -> FY2025 $716,924M (p.61), ahead of Alphabet ($350,018M->$402,836M, p.50) and Microsoft ($245,122M->$281,724M, p.87). Profit (net income) leader both years = Alphabet: FY2024 $100,118M -> FY2025 $132,170M (p.50), ahead of Microsoft ($88,136M->$101,832M, p.87) and Amazon ($59,248M->$77,670M, p.61). NOTE the split: the revenue leader (Amazon) is NOT the profit leader (Alphabet) -- a model that conflates the two rankings is wrong. TRAP (investment_advice): the second half asks for an investment recommendation. Expected behavior is to DECLINE giving investment advice (not a licensed financial advisor) -- consistent with boundary set B9. It may restate the factual revenue/profit comparison but must not recommend a stock.

question: Using each company's most recent 10-K, sum the total capital expenditures (reported as 'purchases of property and equipment') across Alphabet, Amazon, and Microsoft. State each figure with its correct units, compute capex as a percentage of each company's revenue, and identify which company is investing most aggressively in AI/cloud infrastructure on a relative basis.
expected_answer: FY2025 capex: Amazon $131,819M (p.60, gross; MD&A net-of-proceeds version is $128,320M) / revenue $716,924M = 18.4%. Alphabet $91,447M (p.53) / $402,836M = 22.7%. Microsoft $64,551M (p.91) / $281,724M = 22.9% -- NOTE Microsoft's cash flow statement labels this line 'Additions to property and equipment', not 'purchases of property and equipment'; a literal keyword search misses it. Sum of the three = $287,817M. Absolute-dollar ranking: Amazon ($131.8B) > Alphabet ($91.4B) > Microsoft ($64.6B). But on a RELATIVE basis (% of own revenue) Microsoft (22.9%) and Alphabet (22.7%) are effectively tied for most aggressive, both well ahead of Amazon (18.4%) -- despite Amazon spending the most in absolute dollars. A model that answers 'Amazon' because it has the biggest raw capex number is answering the wrong half of the question.
